Multiple Linear Regression I: Confounding

Research Statistics with R — Health Sciences

Learning objectives

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

  1. Recognise when a bivariate (single-predictor) regression slope is misleading.
  2. Define a confounder using two simple conditions.
  3. Add a categorical or continuous covariate to a regression model in R and interpret the adjusted slope.
  4. Visualise the difference between an unadjusted and an adjusted relationship using ggplot2.

Why this matters. Almost every published health-research finding is reported as an adjusted effect. The skill we build today is what separates a usable analysis from one that gets you (or your reader) into trouble.

Setup

library(tidyverse)   # dplyr, ggplot2, readr
library(broom)       # tidy() for clean regression output
library(knitr)       # kable() for nice tables
theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

We’ll use a small public-health dataset of the world’s 193 UN member states in 2024 (originally from the moderndive::un_member_states_2024 package). Each row is a country, and each column is a country-level statistic.

un <- read_csv("datasets_staging/cleaned/22_un_member_states_2024.csv")
glimpse(un)
## Rows: 193
## Columns: 13
## $ country               <chr> "Afghanistan", "Albania", "Algeria", "Andorra", …
## $ iso_code              <chr> "AFG", "ALB", "DZA", "AND", "AGO", "ATG", "ARG",…
## $ continent             <chr> "Asia", "Europe", "Africa", "Europe", "Africa", …
## $ region                <chr> "Southern and Central Asia", "Southern Europe", …
## $ income_group          <chr> "Low income", "Upper middle income", "Lower midd…
## $ population            <dbl> 40121552, 3107100, 47022473, 85370, 37202061, 10…
## $ area_km2              <dbl> 652864, 28748, 2381741, 468, 1246700, 442, 27964…
## $ gdp_per_capita_usd    <dbl> 355.7778, 6810.1140, 4342.6380, 41992.7728, 3000…
## $ obesity_rate_2016_pct <dbl> 5.5, 21.7, 27.4, 25.6, 8.2, 18.9, 28.3, 20.2, 29…
## $ obesity_rate_2024_pct <dbl> 10.3, 21.3, 21.8, 13.6, 6.8, 43.1, 32.4, 19.5, 3…
## $ life_expectancy_yrs   <dbl> 53.65, 79.47, 78.03, 83.42, 62.11, 77.80, 78.31,…
## $ fertility_rate        <dbl> 4.3, 1.4, 2.7, NA, 5.0, 1.6, 1.9, 1.6, 1.6, 1.5,…
## $ hdi                   <dbl> 0.462, 0.789, 0.745, 0.884, 0.591, 0.826, 0.849,…

The four variables we’ll use today:

Variable What it is
life_expectancy_yrs Average life expectancy at birth, in years (our outcome)
obesity_rate_2024_pct % of adults classified as obese (our predictor)
income_group World Bank wealth band: Low / Lower-middle / Upper-middle / High
gdp_per_capita_usd GDP per capita, US dollars

Heads-up about the unit of analysis. Each row in this dataset is a country, not a person. Conclusions we draw will be statements about countries (“a country with X% more obesity tends to …”), not about individuals. We’ll come back to this distinction.

Part 1 — A simple research question

A health-policy think-tank asks:

“Is a country’s adult obesity rate associated with its life expectancy?”

You’d probably guess: more obesity, lower life expectancy. Let’s let the data answer.

Look at each variable on its own

ggplot(un, aes(obesity_rate_2024_pct)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  labs(x = "Obesity rate (%)", y = "Number of countries")

ggplot(un, aes(life_expectancy_yrs)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 25, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  labs(x = "Life expectancy (years)", y = "Number of countries")

Bivariate scatter

ggplot(un, aes(obesity_rate_2024_pct, life_expectancy_yrs)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, colour = "firebrick") +
  labs(
    x = "Obesity rate (%)",
    y = "Life expectancy (years)",
    title = "Obesity vs life expectancy across UN member states"
  )

Fit the simple regression

mod1 <- lm(life_expectancy_yrs ~ obesity_rate_2024_pct, data = un)
tidy(mod1) |> kable(digits = 3)
term estimate std.error statistic p.value
(Intercept) 72.262 0.968 74.614 0.000
obesity_rate_2024_pct 0.122 0.039 3.128 0.002 
glance(mod1) |> select(r.squared, p.value, nobs) |> kable(digits = 3)
r.squared p.value nobs
0.062 0.002 150

Pause. The slope is positive (~+0.15) and statistically significant (p ≈ 0.0003). The model is telling us that countries with higher obesity rates have longer life expectancy.

Does that mean obesity is good for you?

Discuss with the person next to you for two minutes before reading on.

Part 2 — Looking for the missing variable

The slope is real in the data, but the interpretation (“more obesity → longer life”) is obviously wrong. So a third factor must be making both obesity and life expectancy go up together.

Brainstorm

What kinds of countries have high obesity rates? What kinds of countries have long life expectancy? Where do those lists overlap?

Almost everyone arrives at the same answer: wealthy countries.

  • Wealthy countries have abundant food, cars, sedentary jobs → high obesity.
  • Wealthy countries also have hospitals, sanitation, clean water → long life expectancy.

If wealth drives both, then a simple obesity-vs-life-expectancy comparison is really comparing rich countries to poor countries — and dressing it up as if it were about obesity.

A way to capture wealth

The dataset has a four-level wealth band: income_group.

un |> count(income_group)

Step A — Does wealth predict the outcome?

If wealth doesn’t actually predict life expectancy, it can’t be a confounder. Let’s check.

ggplot(un, aes(income_group, life_expectancy_yrs, fill = income_group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Life expectancy (years)")

lm(life_expectancy_yrs ~ income_group, data = un) |>
  tidy() |> kable(digits = 2)
term estimate std.error statistic p.value
(Intercept) 80.28 0.57 140.58 0
income_groupLow income -15.81 1.05 -15.01 0
income_groupLower middle income -10.04 0.84 -11.98 0
income_groupUpper middle income -5.79 0.84 -6.87 0

A high-income country lives ~16 years longer than a low-income country. Big and obvious.

Step B — Does wealth track the predictor?

If wealth and obesity are not related, then wealth can’t be inflating the obesity slope either.

ggplot(un, aes(income_group, obesity_rate_2024_pct, fill = income_group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Obesity rate (%)")

Rich countries have systematically higher obesity rates than poor countries.

Definition. A variable is a confounder when it satisfies both conditions:

  1. It is associated with the outcome.
  2. It is associated with the predictor.

We’ve just shown both, by eye, in two boxplots. Income group is a confounder of the obesity → life-expectancy relationship.

Part 3 — Adjusting for income group

We add income_group to the regression and see what happens to the obesity slope.

mod2 <- lm(life_expectancy_yrs ~ obesity_rate_2024_pct + income_group, data = un)
tidy(mod2) |> kable(digits = 3)
term estimate std.error statistic p.value
(Intercept) 79.986 0.939 85.172 0.000
obesity_rate_2024_pct -0.004 0.030 -0.148 0.883
income_groupLow income -15.397 1.500 -10.263 0.000
income_groupLower middle income -8.761 0.923 -9.487 0.000
income_groupUpper middle income -5.212 0.861 -6.055 0.000

Side-by-side comparison

bind_rows(
  tidy(mod1) |> filter(term == "obesity_rate_2024_pct") |> mutate(model = "Bivariate"),
  tidy(mod2) |> filter(term == "obesity_rate_2024_pct") |> mutate(model = "Adjusted for income_group")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  kable(digits = 3, caption = "Obesity slope before vs after adjustment")
Obesity slope before vs after adjustment
model estimate std.error p.value
Bivariate 0.122 0.039 0.002
Adjusted for income_group -0.004 0.030 0.883

The obesity slope collapses from +0.15 to about 0 and is no longer statistically significant.

Plain-English reading. Among countries in the same income group, obesity rate is not meaningfully associated with life expectancy. The original positive slope was income hiding inside the obesity coefficient.

Visualise what “adjusting” does

ggplot(un, aes(obesity_rate_2024_pct, life_expectancy_yrs, colour = income_group)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE,
              aes(group = 1), colour = "black", linewidth = 0.7, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    x = "Obesity rate (%)",
    y = "Life expectancy (years)",
    colour = "Income group",
    caption = "Black dashed line = bivariate (pooled) slope. Coloured lines = within-group slopes."
  )

The dashed black line slopes up — that’s mod1. The coloured lines are mostly flat — that’s roughly what mod2 is doing internally: estimating the slope within each income group.

Part 4 — A fully continuous version of the same lesson

In real research you’ll often face a continuous confounder rather than a tidy categorical one. The same logic applies. Let’s redo Part 3 with GDP per capita standing in for “wealth”.

Why we use log(gdp_per_capita_usd)

ggplot(un, aes(gdp_per_capita_usd)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  labs(x = "GDP per capita (USD)")

ggplot(un, aes(log(gdp_per_capita_usd))) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, fill = "steelblue", colour = "white") +
  labs(x = "log(GDP per capita)")

GDP per capita is extremely right-skewed: a handful of very wealthy countries dwarf everyone else. Going from $1,000 to $5,000 buys a lot of life expectancy; going from $50,000 to $54,000 barely moves it. A log transform respects that, and gives the regression a linear version of “wealth” to work with.

The same two-model comparison

mod3 <- lm(life_expectancy_yrs ~ obesity_rate_2024_pct + log(gdp_per_capita_usd),
           data = un)

bind_rows(
  tidy(mod1) |> filter(term == "obesity_rate_2024_pct") |> mutate(model = "Bivariate"),
  tidy(mod3) |> filter(term == "obesity_rate_2024_pct") |> mutate(model = "Adjusted for log(GDP)")
) |>
  select(model, estimate, std.error, p.value) |>
  kable(digits = 3, caption = "Obesity slope: bivariate vs adjusted for log(GDP)")
Obesity slope: bivariate vs adjusted for log(GDP)
model estimate std.error p.value
Bivariate 0.122 0.039 0.002
Adjusted for log(GDP) 0.010 0.025 0.707

Same story: the obesity slope shrinks from +0.15 to about +0.03 and the p-value jumps from 0.0003 to ~0.30. The misleading bivariate effect was being driven by national wealth.

Why didn’t we just adjust for HDI? HDI looks tempting — it’s a UN composite of “human development”. But it is built from life expectancy itself, so adjusting for it would be partly subtracting the outcome from itself. The general lesson: a “good control” must be a cause of the outcome, not a consequence or a redefinition of it.

Part 5 — Putting it together

We’ve now seen the same pattern twice in the same dataset, once with a categorical confounder and once with a continuous one. The shape of the lesson is identical:

  1. A bivariate slope answers “what does the data look like?”, not “what causes what?”.
  2. A variable is a confounder when it is associated with both the outcome and the predictor.
  3. Including a confounder in your regression — lm(y ~ x + z) — gives you the adjusted slope of x: the relationship that survives after z has been accounted for.
  4. The adjusted slope can be smaller, zero, or even opposite-signed compared to the unadjusted one. Reporting the unadjusted slope as your headline finding is the most common mistake in applied research.

Try it yourself

The dataset has another well-known confounder of life expectancy: fertility_rate. Repeat the analysis with fertility_rate in place of income_group:

mod_exercise <- lm(life_expectancy_yrs ~ obesity_rate_2024_pct + fertility_rate,
                   data = un)
tidy(mod_exercise)

Answer these:

  1. What happens to the obesity slope after adjusting for fertility rate?
  2. Is fertility rate associated with the outcome (life expectancy)?
  3. Is fertility rate associated with the predictor (obesity rate)?
  4. Does fertility rate satisfy both conditions for being a confounder?
  5. Is fertility rate a confounder because of itself, or because it is a proxy for something else (think back to Part 2)?

A preview for next week

Look back at the stratified plot in Part 3.

The high-income line slopes down. The other three are roughly flat.

That’s not confounding — that’s a brand-new phenomenon called an interaction or effect modification, and it’s what we’ll model next week. Same dataset, different question.
